rubric_notes: Each criterion acts as an atomic reasoning step reflecting the professor's strict grading notes. Exact point deductions from the provided graded exam are mapped to discrete criteria (e.g., Q6 penalizes lack of DP memoization, Q7 penalizes extensive brute-force algorithms).

questions.grading_note: Award up to 4 points per correct technique identified, prorated by the number of valid example problems provided (1 point per valid problem).

criteria.description: Identifies Divide and Conquer and lists 4 valid problems (e.g., merge sort, quick sort, closest pair)
Identifies Greedy and lists 4 valid problems (e.g., interval scheduling, course scheduling, MST)
Identifies Dynamic Programming and lists 4 valid problems (e.g., rod cutting, knapsack)

questions.grading_note: Points are split evenly between the Bellman-Ford and Dijkstra algorithms (20 points total per algorithm).

criteria.description: Bellman-Ford: Explains logic correctly (initializing distances to source as 0 and others to infinity, then slowly updating/relaxing distances)
Dijkstra: Explains logic correctly (greedy approach, initializing distances, and choosing the best vertex based on current known distance)
Bellman-Ford: Identifies advantage (can handle negative edges and cycles)
Dijkstra: Identifies advantage (faster execution)
Bellman-Ford: Identifies disadvantage (slower execution because it searches thoroughly)
Dijkstra: Identifies disadvantage (cannot handle negative edges/cycles because it relies on a greedy aspect)
Answers Yes and clearly explains how the two pointers traverse the arrays without skipping elements, checking all necessary combinations in O(m+n)
Answers Yes and explains that the recursive midpoint and base-case structure guarantees all elements in array A are matched against array B via binary search
Correctly answers No
Provides the correct time complexity O(n log m) and explains that the O(log m) binary search runs for all n elements of array A

questions.grading_note: Evaluate the student's solution against ONLY ONE of the two valid algorithmic approaches below: Track A (DFS + Memoization) OR Track B (Sorting + Bottom-Up DP). Choose the track that best matches the student's logic. Do not combine points from both tracks. A highly inefficient brute-force attempt receives maximum 5 points.

criteria.description: Track A (DFS+Memo) - State Exploration: Establishes standard Depth-First Search logic exploring valid 4-directional moves that are strictly strictly greater/smaller than the current cell.

Track A (DFS+Memo) - Memoization: Implements a DP table/memo dictionary to cache results, returning the cached value immediately if a cell has already been visited to prevent exponential re-evaluations.
Track A (DFS+Memo) - Complexity: Achieves or explicitly states an optimal time complexity of O(m*n).
Track B (Sorting) - Initialization & Sorting: Correctly extracts all cells into a list (storing value, row, col) and sorts them by value.
Track B (Sorting) - DP State Updates: Iterates through the sorted list and checks valid 4-directional neighbors, updating a DP table by adding 1 to the path length of the valid neighboring cells.
Track B (Sorting) - Complexity: Recognizes that the time complexity is bottlenecked by the sorting step, stating it as O(mn log(mn)).

questions.grading_note: Evaluate the student's solution against ONLY ONE of the two scenarios below. If the student uses an optimal Dynamic Programming approach, evaluate using the DP criteria (q7_dp_1 to q7_dp_5) for up to 35 points. If the student uses a brute-force Exhaustive Search/Backtracking approach, evaluate using the ES criteria (q7_es_1 to q7_es_4) for a maximum of 15 points. Do not combine points from both scenarios.

criteria.description: DP - State Definition: Correctly identifies the optimal DP state by tracking the height difference between the two supports (e.g., mapping the height difference to the maximum height of the taller support).

DP - State Transitions: Correctly iterates through the rods and calculates new states based on the three valid choices for each rod: (1) not using the rod, (2) adding the rod to the taller support, or (3) adding the rod to the shorter support.
DP - Initialization: Correctly initializes the base case before iteration (e.g., setting the initial difference of 0 to have a maximum height of 0).

DP - Result & Path Reconstruction: Correctly identifies the final max height at a difference of 0 AND includes logic (e.g., history tracking or state pointers) to reconstruct the two specific rod subsets.
DP - Time Complexity: Correctly identifies or derives the time complexity of the DP approach as O(N * S), where N is the number of rods and S is the maximum possible sum/height.
Exhaustive Search - Recursive Logic: Correctly sets up a recursive/backtracking function that explores all three branches for each rod (add to subset 1, add to subset 2, or skip).
Exhaustive Search - Base Case & Validation: Correctly implements the base case when all rods are processed, verifying if subset 1 and subset 2 have an equal sum/height.

Exhaustive Search - Tracking & Result: Correctly maintains the running subsets during recursion and successfully updates/returns the global maximum height and the associated sets when a valid combination is found.
Exhaustive Search - Time Complexity: Correctly identifies or derives the time complexity of the exhaustive search approach as O(3^N) because each rod has three possible states.
